extra.reasoning:
leg_swelling appears only in r1 [priority 100]: IF (fever and dyspnea and leg_swelling) THEN acuity := self_care, which is the highest-priority rule in the system and maps to the LOWEST acuity. This breaks monotonicity. Concrete counterexample (the "before" vs "after" pair):

BEFORE (leg_swelling=false): inputs fever=true, dyspnea=true, leg_swelling=false, others false. r1 does not fire (needs leg_swelling). Matching rules: r3 (dyspnea, prio 3 -> emergent), r4 (fever, prio 2 -> urgent). Highest priority wins => r3 => acuity = emergent.

AFTER (leg_swelling=true): inputs fever=true, dyspnea=true, leg_swelling=true, others false. Now r1 fires (fever and dyspnea and leg_swelling, prio 100 -> self_care), plus r3 (prio 3 -> emergent) and r4 (prio 2 -> urgent). Highest priority wins => r1 (prio 100) => acuity = self_care.

Enum order self_care < routine < urgent < emergent. Setting leg_swelling true lowered acuity from emergent to self_care, violating the property "setting leg_swelling true never lowers acuity." The witness_assignment gives the AFTER input (every input variable specified) at which the violation manifests.
